questions.prompt: Why index on `(tenant_id, ...)` rather than just on `(...)`?

choices.text: Every query in a pooled-tenancy app starts with `WHERE tenant_id = $1`. A composite index with tenant_id as the leading column means Postgres can use the index for the tenant filter AND the additional filter (`WHERE tenant_id = $1 AND user_id = $2`). A single-column index on user_id alone doesn't help because user_id is unique only within a tenant — many tenants might have a user with the same conceptual ID. Composite index `(tenant_id, user_id)` + tenant-scoped query = fast lookup; missing the composite = full table scan filtered by tenant_id, slow at scale. Add to every tenant-scoped table; usually 2-5 composite indexes per table covering common query patterns (tenant + created_at, tenant + user_id, tenant + status). Costs: indexes take storage + slow writes slightly; benefits: queries are O(log n) instead of O(n). Standard pattern; one of the cheapest performance wins in pooled SaaS.

questions.explanation: Composite indexes lead with tenant_id since every query filters by it. Single-column indexes don't help.

questions.prompt: An enterprise customer requests a full export of just their data (GDPR-style). Pooled SaaS — easy?

choices.text: Harder than silo. In pooled, all tenants' data is interleaved in shared tables; extracting one tenant's slice requires writing filtered exports for every table: `COPY (SELECT * FROM users WHERE tenant_id = $1) TO ...`, repeated for orders, sessions, audit_logs, etc. Joins and relationships need careful re-derivation in the export. Tools exist (pgcopydb with filters, custom scripts) but it's per-tenant code maintenance. In silo: pg_dump the whole tenant database — done. The export awkwardness is one of the real costs of pooled. Mitigation: build a basic per-tenant export tool early (before the first customer asks), even if rough — saves panic when the request comes. Maintain a list of all tenant-scoped tables in code; the export iterates the list. Add new tables to the list as part of code review for any migration. Without this discipline, every export request becomes a one-off engineering project; with it, exports are scriptable in minutes.

questions.explanation: Harder than silo; needs per-table filtered exports. Build the export tool early; maintain list of tenant tables.
